464da9fa87b033fbc74a.text:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member onboarding** – no documented first-contact-to-first-class funnel (discovery call → payment setup → induction → first class assignment)
- **Churn & win-back** – no process for pause requests, cancellation reasons capture, or reactivation triggers
- **Coaching standards & QA** – no class quality audit, coaching development roadmap, or weekly task standardisation
- **Member retention mechanics** – no check-in cadence, milestone recognition, 30-day goal-setting, or outcomes tracking
- **Referral programme** – no mechanics, incentive structure, or tracking workflow
- **Data governance** – GDPR & member privacy compliance not documented (critical for women's-only space + payment data)
- **Supply chain** – no sundries/equipment ordering process (impacts class experience, revenue if selling protein/merch)
- **New programme launches** – no quarterly rollout SOP (coaching brief, member comms, class schedule changes, promotion timing)
- **Incident & complaints** – no formal log, response time, or resolution SOP (safeguarding risk in women's space)

---

# **Needs updating**

- **Coaches SOPs (20/24)** – likely missing specifics on: sub cover protocol, late arrivals, sub-standard performance feedback, how to handle difficult members, energy/pacing standards per class type
- **Clients SOPs (23/33)** – gaps probably in: new vs returning member flows, membership tier differences, class booking rules, cancellation windows, payment failures
- **Member communication** – no documented cadence for welcome, re-engagement, expiry reminders, or seasonal campaigns
- **Facilities (5/6)** – missing: deep-clean schedule, equipment maintenance logs, emergency procedures (fire, injury, safeguarding concern), noise/

ef78fa4eea1cbb63abd4.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO ADVICE

## REVENUE — URGENT PRIORITY

• **Churn spike is killing growth.** June hit 0% attrition (excellent), but May-June revenue *fell* £2,488 (–12.3%) despite adding 25 members (May 122 → June 147). You're acquiring new members but losing paying revenue per member: AR/M dropped from £165.56 to £120.48 (–27.2%). **Action:** Audit your pricing tiers immediately. Members joining at lower price points or on intro offers are dragging average revenue down. Implement a January price rise on renewals and shift new sign-ups to higher-tier memberships (personal training packages, premium classes, nutrition add-ons).

• **Quiet-class capacity problem post-relocation.** You've doubled studio size but revenue is flat. May (£20,198) and June (£17,710) are both below December peak (£21,133) despite 22 more active members. **Action:** Map class utilisation by time slot and instructor. Run 2–3 off-peak classes at dynamic pricing (happy-hour rates) or convert lowest-attendance slots to premium small-group sessions (PT+2 format at £25–30/head). Test a Saturday morning retention class or weekday lunchtime corporate package.

• **Upsell and new revenue lines underdeveloped.** Relocation was meant to unlock scale. **Action:** Launch a retail corner (protein, water bottles, apparel—30% margin), introduce nutrition coaching (£50–75/month recurring), run paid challenge events (6-week transformation at £120/person—high-margin group revenue). Track uptake separately from membership.

---

## EXPENSES — SECOND PRIORITY

• **Non-people costs are volatile and overrunning.** June non-people spend: £9,390. May: £9,233. Excluding the April relocation anomaly, Feb–Mar averaged £13,277/month (crisis period—likely one-off lease/utility spike post-move). June should be **clean**, but it's still £9,390 on only £17,710 revenue (53% of revenue vs May's 46%). **Action:** Itemise that £9,390: isolate utilities, rent, insurance, software. New studio is larger—expect higher rent/energy—but £9k monthly is unsustainable at current revenue. Negotiate lease terms, switch utility supplier, audit software subscriptions (kill unused tools). Target: cap non-people expenses at 40% of revenue (= £7,000 at June volumes).

• **

f527670fd10b0672288e.text:
• **JoJo is overloaded at 133 sessions.** Redistribute her Build: Upper classes (15/month) to Eilis, and move some Circuits (6/month) to either Eilis or a new hire. This brings JoJo down to ~110 sessions and creates capacity for her to mentor and lead the team strategically.

• **Eilis is under-utilised at 48 sessions and ready for more responsibility.** Assign her Grace's Build: Lower classes (17/month) plus JoJo's Build: Upper overflow. This grows Eilis to ~70 sessions, develops her confidence, and reduces single-coach dependency on JoJo.

• **Grace's PT load (44 sessions from JoJo + 11 from Eilis = 55 PT sessions/month) must be shared.** Redistribute her 44 PT clients: assign ~25 to JoJo (her existing clients), ~15 to Eilis, and ~4 to a part-time coach or junior hire. This is realistic client handover, not an impossible spike for any one person.

• **No Sweat Intro and Strongevity classes (6 sessions/month combined) are at risk.** Eilis should take No Sweat Intro once confident; assign Strongevity to JoJo or consider a specialist or hybrid coach to cover niche programming and prevent burnout on core classes.

• **Hire a part-time coach (8–12 hours/week) for Circuits, Plate, Capacity, and Body Composition scans.** This protects JoJo and Eilis from admin-heavy scanning, covers lower-intensity classes, and gives the team breathing room while Grace fully exits delivery.

**Filling quiet studio time:**

1. **Add a 30-min "Open Strength" slot on Monday/Thursday evenings** — these are your quietest days and members often want flexible gym access between structured classes; charge £3-5 per session or include in membership to boost studio utilization.

2. **Host a monthly "Technique Workshop" (45 min) in the mid-afternoon quiet window** — focus on form for underperforming classes like Barbelle and Engine; market it as prerequisite/confidence-builder for members hesitant about those formats.

**Lifting quieter classes:**

3. **Move Barbelle and Strongevity to Tuesday or Wednesday mornings** (your peak days) and promote them as "complement to Build classes" — members already in for Upper/Full Body lifts may grab a second session; simultaneously trial a 6am or 7:45am slot to capture commuters before work.

4. **Rebrand Engine as "30-min cardio finisher" and slot it immediately *after* a Build class** — offer members a 10-min transition window; this converts idle post-class time into another paid class rather than asking them to commit to a standalone slot they're avoiding.

47b0548900970eee6400.text:
# Service Menu Execution for The Forge

**1. Start with your coaches, not members**
Have a 30-min 1-on-1 with each coach this week. Ask: "What do you actually sell?" (not what you think you sell). You'll find gaps, overlaps, and hidden offerings. Document their real language—use it in your menu. This prevents a beautiful menu that confuses your team.

**2. Price anchoring first, then packaging**
Don't start with "what should this cost?" Start with: What's your highest-margin service? What's your stickiest? Price that first at market-rate or slightly above. Then work backwards—your budget options become "entry points" to that premium tier. This prevents underpricing.

**3. Create two menus, same content, different frames**
**Coach menu:** lists what they deliver, how long it takes, what they need from members (effort level, consistency, etc.). **Member menu:** benefits-focused, removes jargon, includes testimonials. One sentence per service max. Test the member version on 3 non-customers first—if they're confused, rewrite.

**4. Embed decision-making into your pricing**
Include a simple decision tree: "New to strength training? → This package. Already lifting, want a program? → This one." Reduces objection friction and makes upsells feel natural, not pushy.

**5. Audit what you're *actually* delivering**
Before publishing, secretly audit 3 random member experiences. Does the menu match reality? If not, fix the delivery or rewrite the menu. Misalignment kills trust and repeat business.

What's your current highest revenue service?

e4aeda2aa0d1508a8f79.text:
**INSIGHTS**

1. **You've cracked the code in July.** Cost-per-lead dropped 10x (£21 to £2) and you finally converted leads to sales (22 at 13% close rate). This isn't noise—something fundamental shifted in creative, targeting, or messaging. April–June were essentially dead money; July is your baseline for what's possible.

2. **The efficiency is real but fragile.** £2 CPL and £60 profit per sale looks strong, but you're running at breakeven on ad spend alone (£376 spend → £31 income = 8% ROAS). You're profitable only because of the 13% close rate. If that drops, you're underwater fast. If it holds and scales, you can 2–3x budget immediately.

3. **You've solved demand generation, not yet demand capture.** 165 leads in one month after months of 20–69 tells us July's targeting and creative *finally* attracted the right audience. But 87% still didn't convert—that's either a sales/follow-up gap, or the leads are warm but not sales-ready on day one.

---

**ACTIONS**

1. **Immediately audit and document what changed in July.** Pull creative assets, audiences, placements, and copy used last month vs June. Was there a new video? Different audience narrowing? Seasonal shift? LCA change? You need to know *exactly* which variable caused the 10x CPL drop, because that's your repeatable engine. If you can't identify it, you got lucky—and luck doesn't scale.

2. **Stress-test the close rate before scaling spend.** 22 sales from 165 leads is excellent, but small sample. Run the same creative/targeting for another 2 weeks at current budget (£180 spend). If you hit ~11–13% close again, you have proof of concept and can safely double budget. If it drops to 5–7%, the July cohort was anomalous (wrong audience, wrong timing, or a one-off message match). Budget guidance: *Do not increase spend until you've confirmed the close rate holds at scale.*

3. **Split-test the 13% close rate

fbb52f88c48e54607ffa.text:
# The Forge — Fractional CFO Action Plan

## INCREASE REVENUE

• **Price your membership.** Rev/member is volatile (£119–£138) but stuck below £130 most months. At 134 members (June), you're leaving £1,300–£2,000/month on the table vs your best (Feb/April at £130–£138). Raise standard membership by £5–10/month immediately — your churn at 3.3% (June) shows pricing power. Target: £135+ per member by month-end.

• **Plug the churn spike in May (12.7%) and December (11.8%).** These aren't random. Investigate: Are class schedules dropping in those months? Are you running retention campaigns post-New Year? Run a post-cancellation survey in the next 48 hours for May churners (you should still have email trails). Typical gym churn is 5–7%; you're at 3–6% most months but spiking hard twice yearly — fix the trigger and you hold 8–10 extra members per cycle = £960–£1,200 recurring.

• **Launch class-based add-ons and PT.** Revenue stuck at £14k–£17k/month on 119–135 members. You have a captive, engaged audience (women-only niche is defensible). Introduce £20/month "unlimited PT drop-ins" or £15 "bootcamp pass" as upsell to base membership. Even 20% take-up = £350–£400/month new revenue, zero extra fixed cost initially.

## REDUCE EXPENSES

• **March and June cash burn is structural.** Excluding April (relocation), you've burned £5,307 (March) and £2,521 (June) against modest revenue swings. Examine: Are rent or rates spikes quarterly? Are utility/maintenance hitting in those months? Pull your last 12 months of supplier invoices (Starling export) and flag anything non-payroll over £1,500. Post-relocation, your fixed cost footprint may have jumped — if March/June burns repeat, you've likely overscaled the studio or have a contract issue.

• **Staffing transparency needed.** You've not given me payroll, but at £14k–£16k revenue, if staff + rent exceed 65% of revenue, you're underwater. June revenue was £15,765; if cash out (£20,644) includes all staff, supplier, utilities and rent, you're spending 131% of revenue — unsustainable. Itemise: What's your combined staff + rent monthly?
